paper_metadata.authors: Agarwal, N., Moehring, A., Rajpurkar, P., & Salz, T.
paper_metadata.title: Combining Human Expertise with Artificial Intelligence: Experimental Evidence from Radiology
paper_metadata.sample: 227 professional radiologists recruited through teleradiology companies

productivity.finding: On average, AI assistance does not improve radiologists' diagnostic accuracy and slightly increases the time taken, with performance improving only when the AI is highly confident and worsening when it is uncertain.

substitute_complement.finding: Due to human biases in incorporating AI advice, the optimal system delegates tasks to either the AI or the human alone, suggesting AI acts as a substitute for human labor on diagnostic tasks rather than a complement.

findings.comparison: Access to clinical history vs no access
findings.metric_definition: The absolute difference between the radiologist's reported probability of a pathology and the diagnostic standard (a consensus of experts). A reduction indicates improved performance.

findings.comparison: AI assistance vs no AI
findings.metric_definition: The absolute difference between the radiologist's reported probability of a pathology and the diagnostic standard (a consensus of experts). A positive coefficient indicates worse performance.
findings.note: The effect is not statistically significant (p > 0.05). The control mean deviation is 0.226.

findings.comparison: AI assistance vs no AI
findings.metric_definition: Time in seconds spent on a case.
findings.note: The effect is statistically significant (t=2.53 based on Table C.6).

result.one_line: In an experiment with 227 radiologists, providing an AI diagnostic tool did not improve accuracy on average and slightly increased time spent per case, as radiologists underweighted the AI's advice and failed to account for correlations between their own and the AI's information.
result.qual_notes: This study used a field experiment with professional radiologists in a 2x2 design, varying access to an AI diagnostic tool (a non-generative image classifier) and patient clinical history. The main finding is that while the AI was more accurate than 78% of participants, its assistance did not improve average performance due to systematic human biases in belief updating (automation neglect and signal dependence neglect). The authors conclude that the optimal system would delegate cases entirely to either the human or the AI, rather than promoting human-AI collaboration on the same task.

paper_metadata.title: Artificial Intelligence and Groups: Effects of Attitudes and Discretion on Collaboration
paper_metadata.sample: N/A (Conceptual paper)

productivity.finding: AI can augment team decision-making and free up members for other tasks, but the ultimate effect on collaboration and performance depends on team attitudes and whether AI use is voluntary or mandatory.

substitute_complement.finding: AI can act as a teammate that either complements humans by augmenting decision-making or substitutes for them by automating processes, which can remove team members' autonomy.

result.one_line: The effect of AI on team collaboration is asymmetric, depending on the interaction between a team's pre-existing attitude toward AI and whether its use is voluntary or mandated by the organization.
result.qual_notes: This conceptual paper develops a theoretical framework proposing that the effect of AI on team collaboration depends on the interaction between team attitudes (positive/negative) and implementation discretion (voluntary/mandatory). The framework posits that collaboration is highest when positive-attitude teams voluntarily use AI and lowest when negative-attitude teams have a choice. Mandated AI use is theorized to produce medium collaboration levels in both cases, suggesting it can elevate engagement for resistant teams but suppress it for enthusiastic ones; the paper does not empirically test these propositions.

paper_metadata.title: Lawyering in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
paper_metadata.sample: 59 law students at the University of Minnesota Law School

productivity.finding: Generative AI (GPT-4) substantially increased the speed of completing legal tasks (12-32% time reduction) but only slightly and inconsistently improved the quality of the work.

inequality.finding: AI assistance disproportionately benefited lower-skilled participants, compressing the performance gap between high and low performers on tasks where AI was most useful.

entry_level.finding: AI can substantially improve the efficiency of law students performing tasks typical of junior attorneys, particularly in speed.

substitute_complement.finding: The study models AI as a complement to human labor, finding that AI assistance can significantly improve the productivity of law students on realistic legal tasks.

result.one_line: In a randomized trial with law students, GPT-4 assistance substantially reduced the time to complete legal tasks (by 12-32%) and compressed performance gaps, but only slightly and inconsistently improved output quality.
result.qual_notes: The study was a randomized controlled trial with 59 law students from the University of Minnesota, who completed four realistic legal tasks (drafting a complaint, contract, employee handbook section, and client memo). The quality improvements were task-dependent, with the largest gains on contract drafting, while time savings were consistent across all tasks. The AI's benefit to quality was concentrated among lower-performing students, indicating a skill-leveling effect. The authors note the sample is small and from a single elite law school, which may limit generalizability.

paper_metadata.sample: 33 university students participating in a lab experiment

productivity.finding: AI teammates that decrease their influence over time significantly improve human performance, whereas AIs that increase their influence stagnate human performance improvement.

inequality.finding: Qualitative findings suggest personal motivations (e.g., desire to win vs. learn by doing) determine preferences for AI influence, which may correlate with player skill, but this was not quantitatively tested.

substitute_complement.finding: Highly influential AI teammates are perceived as substituting for human players and acting like unpleasant 'ball hogs', while less influential AIs are seen as complementing human action and enabling learning.

result.one_line: In a team-based game, AI teammates that gradually reduce their influence over time significantly improve human player performance, while AIs that increase their influence stifle human improvement.
result.qual_notes: This lab experiment (N=33) had participants play a team-based video game (Rocket League) with an AI teammate whose 'influence' (how often it would take the ball) was varied. The key finding is that an AI that starts with high influence and then decreases it over time leads to the largest improvement in human performance. Qualitative results suggest this 'scaffolding' approach allows humans to first learn by watching a competent AI and then provides them space to practice and learn by doing, whereas a constantly high-influence AI is perceived as a 'ball hog' that demotivates human players.

paper_metadata.title: Experimental evidence on the productivity effects of generative artificial intelligence
paper_metadata.sample: 453 college-educated professionals in marketing, grant writing, consulting, data analysis, HR, and management

productivity.finding: ChatGPT substantially increased productivity for midlevel professional writing tasks, decreasing the average time taken by 40% and increasing output quality by 18%.

inequality.finding: ChatGPT decreased performance inequality by providing greater benefits to lower-skilled workers, reducing the correlation between a worker's initial and final task performance by more than half.

substitute_complement.finding: In this experimental setting, workers used ChatGPT primarily as a substitute for their own effort, submitting its output with minimal editing, though the authors speculate it may be more of a complement in more complex, real-world tasks.

result.one_line: In an experiment with 453 professionals, access to ChatGPT decreased the time spent on writing tasks by 40% and increased output quality by 18%, with lower-skilled workers benefiting the most, thus reducing performance inequality.
result.qual_notes: This study was a preregistered, randomized controlled trial with 453 college-educated professionals performing occupation-specific writing tasks for financial incentives. The tasks were short, self-contained, and did not require external or context-specific knowledge, which may represent an ideal use-case for ChatGPT and limit generalizability to more complex real-world work. Follow-up surveys showed that workers in the treatment group were significantly more likely to adopt ChatGPT in their real jobs two weeks and two months after the experiment.

inequality.finding: Less experienced programmers benefited more from using GitHub Copilot, suggesting the tool helps compress performance gaps between novices and experts.

entry_level.finding: By disproportionately benefiting novice programmers, the AI tool shows promise in supporting job transitions and expanding access to software development careers.

result.qual_notes: This study was a randomized controlled trial with 95 professional freelance developers tasked with writing an HTTP server in JavaScript. The primary outcome was a 55.8% reduction in completion time for the group with access to GitHub Copilot, with no significant effect on task success rate. The paper also finds heterogeneous effects, with less experienced programmers, older programmers, and those who code more hours per day benefiting the most, suggesting the AI tool may help compress skill gaps.

result.one_line: In a lab experiment with 181 IT administrators, access to a generative AI security copilot increased accuracy by 35% and reduced task completion time by 30%, with larger gains on more complex, open-ended tasks.
result.qual_notes: The study was a randomized controlled trial where IT administrators performed simulated tasks with or without access to Microsoft's Security Copilot. The productivity benefits were largest for complex, free-response tasks (+146% accuracy, -61% time) and smallest for multiple-choice tasks, where AI users were actually slower. While point estimates suggested that less-experienced workers benefited more in accuracy, these differences were not statistically significant. The study measures the incremental benefit of Copilot, as control subjects were allowed to use other tools like web search or other AIs.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI can substitute for a human crowd in creative idea generation, and a complementary, iterative human-AI partnership ('differentiated search') produces higher-quality solutions than a more independent AI search process.

result.one_line: In a creative problem-solving challenge, business ideas generated by a human-AI partnership were rated as higher in quality and value than those from a human crowd, though the crowd's ideas were rated as more novel.
result.qual_notes: This study conducted a field experiment comparing business ideas for the circular economy generated by a human crowd (from 125 solvers) versus a human using GPT-4. The resulting 234 solutions were rated by 300 external evaluators. While human-crowd solutions were more novel on average and more likely to receive top novelty ratings, human-AI solutions were superior in overall quality, strategic viability, and financial/environmental value. The study also found that an iterative, human-guided 'differentiated search' with the AI produced higher quality outputs than a more independent AI search, highlighting the value of human-AI complementarity.

substitute_complement.finding: AI complements human workers, with the degree of complementarity depending on the worker's ability to accurately assess their own skills (calibration); better-calibrated workers make better use of AI assistance.

result.qual_notes: This lab experiment (N=732) asked participants to guess if a person in a photo was over 21, with or without an AI assistant's prediction. The key finding is that the productivity gains from AI are mediated by 'belief calibration'—workers who accurately know their own strengths and weaknesses benefit more. Low-ability but well-calibrated workers saw the largest gains, suggesting AI can reduce inequality, but miscalibration limits this effect. The AI used was a classification model, not a generative AI.

result.qual_notes: This study was a randomized controlled trial with 355 online participants (experts and non-experts in marketing) tasked with writing ad copy. The key finding is that the modality of AI use is critical: the sounding board modality complemented non-experts, helping them write more like experts, while the ghostwriter modality substituted for expert skill and led to worse, less creative outcomes due to an anchoring effect.

paper_metadata.title: Al-enhanced collective intelligence
paper_metadata.sample: This is a review paper and does not have a sample.

productivity.finding: AI enhances collective intelligence and team performance, with hybrid human-AI teams capable of achieving higher performance metrics than all-human teams.

substitute_complement.finding: AI is framed as a tool to complement and enhance human skills and collective intelligence rather than as a substitute that replaces human capabilities outright.

result.one_line: This review conceptualizes AI as an enhancer of human collective intelligence, arguing that hybrid human-AI teams can achieve superior performance by complementing human skills rather than replacing them.
result.qual_notes: This is a conceptual review paper that synthesizes existing literature to propose a multilayer network framework for understanding AI-enhanced collective intelligence. It does not present original empirical data but instead reviews various factors like group diversity, team structure, and AI's roles (assistant, teammate, coach) that influence the performance of human-AI teams. The paper's focus is on the mechanisms of collaboration and performance enhancement at the team level, not on labor market outcomes.

result.qual_notes: This study is a field experiment at Ant Group involving 1,219 programmers, where a treatment group used the 'CodeFuse' LLM. The primary productivity measure is lines of code produced. The paper finds that productivity gains are almost entirely driven by junior staff (<=1 year experience), as senior staff used the AI tool less frequently, though they found it useful when they did. The majority of the productivity gain (36-43%) is attributed to indirect effects like time savings, rather than direct code generation (11-18%).

paper_metadata.sample: 41.3 million papers and 5.4 million researchers in natural sciences from the OpenAlex dataset

productivity.finding: Scientists using AI publish 3.02 times more papers and receive 4.84 times more citations annually compared to their non-AI-using counterparts.

inequality.finding: AI adoption benefits junior scientists, who are 13.6% more likely to become established researchers and do so 1.37 years faster than their non-AI-adopting peers.

entry_level.finding: AI-augmented research teams have 31.1% fewer junior scientists compared to non-AI teams, a much larger reduction than the 10.8% decrease for established scientists.

substitute_complement.finding: AI appears to substitute for human labor in scientific teams, as AI-augmented research is associated with teams that are 19.3% smaller, with a disproportionate reduction in junior scientists.

result.one_line: In science, AI adoption boosts individual productivity (3x more papers, 5x more citations) and accelerates junior careers, but is associated with smaller teams (especially fewer juniors) and a collective narrowing of research focus.
result.qual_notes: This observational study analyzes over 41 million scientific papers to compare outcomes for scientists who do and do not use AI. The study finds that while AI users are more productive and junior AI users advance faster, AI-augmented teams are smaller, particularly with a 31% reduction in junior scientists. A major secondary finding is that AI use is correlated with a contraction in the 'knowledge extent' of science, suggesting AI focuses research on data-rich, established problems rather than exploring new frontiers. The study's definition of AI use is based on text analysis of papers, and the analysis is correlational, not causal.

evidence.snippet: On average, researchers adopting AI annually publish 3.02 times more papers (t ≥47.18, p < 0.001 and d f > 103 in t-test on any discipline)

and garner 4.84 times more citations (t ≥30.32, p < 0.001 and d f > 103 in t-test on any discipline) compared with those not adopting AI, with consistency across disciplines and robustness for core researchers with multi-year continuous publication records41 (Fig. 2b, Extended Data Fig. 4 and Supplementary Fig. S17).
Specifically, the average number of junior scientists decreased from 2.89 in non-AI teams to 1.99 (31.14%) in AI teams (t = 19.02, p < 0.001 and d f > 103 in t-test),
while the number of established scientists decreased from 4.01 to 3.58 (10.77%) in AI teams (t = 20.82, p < 0.001 and d f > 103 in t-test).
We find that the anticipated transition time to becoming established scientists is 1.37 years shorter for AI-adopted junior scientists compared to their counterparts.
The expectation of transition time is 7.33 years for junior scientists adopting AI (R2 = 0.995) and 8.70 years for those without (R2 = 0.987).

result.one_line: In an online writing experiment, generative AI assistance reduced completion time and errors, particularly for lower-confidence writers, but AI-driven content generation also decreased writer satisfaction, sense of ownership, and the diversity of the final output.
result.qual_notes: This study was a randomized experiment with 379 online workers (on Prolific) assigned to write a short argumentative or creative piece. Participants were in one of three conditions: no AI, AI for editing assistance (complement), or AI for initial draft generation (substitute). The study finds a key trade-off: while content-generating AI increased speed and reduced errors, it also led to lower satisfaction, enjoyment, sense of ownership, and a significant reduction in the diversity of written outputs. The study's context is a low-stakes, one-off task, and the authors note that findings may differ in high-stakes professional or intrinsically motivated writing scenarios.

paper_metadata.title: Unraveling Human-AI Teaming: A Review and Outlook
paper_metadata.sample: N/A (Conceptual paper)

productivity.finding: AI can enhance productivity and problem-solving efficiency by augmenting human capabilities, reducing cognitive workload, and freeing humans from repetitive tasks to focus on higher-level problem-solving.

inequality.finding: Advanced AI may reduce expertise gaps between workers, potentially disadvantaging highly skilled individuals whose unique contributions become less distinct.

substitute_complement.finding: AI is evolving from a passive tool into an active collaborator or teammate, complementing human labor by taking on tasks, flattening team hierarchies, and redistributing expertise.

result.one_line: This review paper proposes a framework for human-AI teaming, arguing that generative AI is evolving from a passive tool to an active collaborator, which requires rethinking team formulation, coordination, and dynamics to realize its full potential.
result.qual_notes: This is a conceptual review and outlook paper that synthesizes existing literature on human-AI teaming and proposes a research agenda based on Team Situation Awareness (SA) theory. It does not present new empirical data but instead develops a framework for future research on human-AI team formulation, coordination, maintenance, and training. The paper argues that viewing AI as an active teammate, rather than just a tool, is crucial for unlocking its collaborative potential.

evidence.snippet: Unraveling Human-Al Teaming: A Review and Outlook*

Bowen Lou
University of Southern California, bowenlou@marshall.usc.edu
Tian Lu
Arizona State University, lutian@asu.edu
T. S. Raghu
Arizona State University, Raghu.Santanam@asu.edu
Yingjie Zhang
Peking University, yingjiezhang@gsm.pku.edu.cn
This paper provides a foundational agenda for future research and practical design of sustainable, high-performing human-AI teams.
The rapid rise of large language models (LLMs) such as Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) has accelerated this transformation.
The promise of AI lies in its ability to integrate into teams to augment human capabilities, improve decision-making, enhance productivity, and drive innovation.
On the one hand, AI frees humans from repetitive but boring tasks, which in turn allows them to allocate more cognitive resources to higher-level problem-solving (Jia et al. 2023).

Similarly, experienced workers' concerns about maintaining their unique contributions must be addressed, as advanced AI may reduce expertise gaps, potentially disadvantaging highly skilled individuals (Brynjolfsson and Mitchell 2017, SimanTov-Nachlieli 2024).

This paper explores the evolution of AI agents from passive tools to active collaborators in human-AI teams, emphasizing their ability to learn, adapt, and operate autonomously in complex environments.

While AI agents can be subject to human control in terms of task assignments and (sub)objectives, their advanced capabilities can disrupt traditional team hierarchies by flattening authority structures and redistributing expertise (Tambe 2025, Wu et al. 2019a).

result.one_line: In a field experiment with Kenyan entrepreneurs, a GPT-4 business assistant increased performance inequality, helping high-performers by ~15% but harming low-performers by ~8%, as only the former could effectively select and implement good advice.
result.qual_notes: This field RCT gave 640 Kenyan entrepreneurs access to a GPT-4 business assistant via WhatsApp. Unlike many studies, it found a null average treatment effect, but significant heterogeneity: high-performers benefited while low-performers were harmed. The authors argue this is because entrepreneurs had high discretion in what to ask and which advice to implement, and only high-performers had the complementary skills to filter good advice from bad. This contrasts with studies on more constrained tasks where AI tends to help novices most.

In this paper, we test the potential of AI in a randomized control trial (RCT) with 640 Kenyan small business entrepreneurs who were randomly assigned to receive access to a GPT-4-powered AI business assistant via WhatsApp.

paper_metadata.title: How much does AI impact development speed? An enterprise-based randomized controlled trial
paper_metadata.sample: 96 full-time Google software engineers

productivity.finding: In a randomized controlled trial with 96 software engineers, using AI coding assistants reduced the time to complete a complex enterprise-grade task by about 21%, controlling for developer experience.

inequality.finding: More experienced developers (higher seniority, more hours coding per day) may benefit more from AI than less experienced ones, though the interaction effects were not statistically significant.

entry_level.finding: On a complex enterprise task, early-career developers did not benefit more from AI than senior developers; in fact, the (non-significant) trend suggested senior developers gained more speed.

result.one_line: In a randomized trial with 96 Google software engineers, AI coding tools reduced task completion time by about 21%, with tentative evidence that more experienced developers benefited more.
result.qual_notes: This was a lab-style randomized controlled trial where 96 full-time Google software engineers completed a standardized, complex, enterprise-grade coding task with or without access to three internal AI-powered features. The primary outcome was time on task. While the main effect on speed was large (a 21% reduction), it was not statistically significant (p=0.086) after including controls. The study also found non-significant trends suggesting that more senior developers and those who code more hours per day benefited more from AI, contrary to some studies that find larger benefits for novices.

The interaction between experimental condition and seniority was not statistically significant (model not shown; p = 0.706), but it was negative, suggesting that participants in our sample with increased levels of seniority were faster with AI than those with lower levels of seniority.

Our randomized controlled trial with 96 full-time Google software engineers contributes to this literature by sharing an estimate of the impact of three AI features on the time developers spent on a complex, enterprise-grade task.

paper_metadata.title: Who Benefits from AI? Self-Selection, Skill Gap, and the Hidden Costs of AI Feedback
paper_metadata.sample: 403,010 game-level observations from 52,251 human players on the lichess.org online chess platform.

productivity.finding: After accounting for users' self-selection into using the tool, AI feedback has no average causal effect on performance improvement or learning.

inequality.finding: Access to AI feedback increases the skill gap because higher-skilled, more motivated individuals are more likely to use it and benefit from it disproportionately.

entry_level.finding: Lower-skilled players, a proxy for novices, benefit significantly less from AI feedback than higher-skilled players, suggesting AI access may disadvantage entry-level individuals by widening the performance gap.

substitute_complement.finding: AI feedback acts as a complement to a user's pre-existing motivation, effort, and skill, rather than a substitute for them, as it does not improve performance for unmotivated individuals.

findings.comparison: Effect of prior AI feedback on current performance (endogeneity corrected)
findings.metric_definition: Game-level move accuracy, calculated by Stockfish 16.
findings.note: The paper's main finding is that after correcting for self-selection, AI feedback has no causal effect on performance. The coefficient from the regression is statistically insignificant and effectively zero.

Confidence intervals reported in the paper are [0.019, 0.023] for low-skilled and [0.034, 0.040] for high-skilled individuals. The effect reflects the combined impact of AI feedback and correlated motivation.

result.one_line: In a study of chess players, access to AI feedback widens the skill gap between high- and low-skilled players, as higher-skilled players are more motivated to use it productively, while the AI has no average causal effect on performance once this self-selection is accounted for.
result.qual_notes: This study analyzes over 400,000 games from 52,000 players on a chess platform to study how endogenous choice to use AI feedback affects learning. Using a control function approach to correct for self-selection, the authors find that apparent performance gains from AI are illusory and driven by user motivation. Higher-skilled, more motivated players are more likely to seek and benefit from AI feedback, which widens the population skill gap. A key limitation is the focus on a single domain (chess) where the AI (Stockfish, a non-generative engine) is super-human and its quality is well-known, which may not generalize to all work settings.

The effect of AI feedback is significantly higher for high-skilled individuals than for low-skilled individuals (based on median split: low = 0.021 [95% CI:-0.004] vs. high = 0.037 [95% CI:-0.005999999999999998]).
As a result of this behavioral pattern, AI increases—rather than decreases—the skill gap in the population.
Merely offering access to AI does not help disengaged or less motivated individuals increase their performance and only works as a complement to effort and preexisting skill.

paper_metadata.authors: Riedl, C., Savage, S., & Zvelebilova, J.
paper_metadata.title: Cognitive Spillover in Human-Al Teams
paper_metadata.sample: Study 1: 97 university subjects; Study 2: 69 subjects in 20 teams

productivity.finding: AI exposure implicitly shapes team cognitive processes like shared language and collective attention, which are necessary precursors for effective collaboration, but the paper does not measure the ultimate effect on team performance or output.

substitute_complement.finding: AI acts as a 'social forcefield' rather than a neutral tool, altering human-human team dynamics by influencing shared language and attention, and can either strengthen or disrupt social cohesion depending on whether it is perceived as an in-group or out-group member.

findings.metric: Likelihood of using AI-specific words in subsequent speech
findings.comparison: AI-exposed words vs. counterfactual words in subsequent human-human speech (Study 1)
findings.metric_definition: The percentage increase in the likelihood that a person will use words they were exposed to by an AI in a later conversation with another human.
findings.note: This measures the 'linguistic spillover' effect from human-AI to subsequent human-human interaction.

findings.comparison: Change in use of AI terminology for core task concepts after AI intervention vs. control terms (DiD, Study 2)
findings.metric_definition: A regression coefficient from a difference-in-differences model measuring the increased adoption of AI's terminology by human teams after the AI has spoken.
findings.note: This effect was strongest in the 'Human-Helpful' condition (β = 0.15) and weaker or insignificant in others.

findings.comparison: Team discussion of AI-mentioned topics vs. non-mentioned topics after AI intervention (DiD, Study 2)
findings.metric_definition: A regression coefficient from a difference-in-differences model measuring the extent to which AI interjections shifted the team's conversational focus.

result.one_line: In two randomized experiments, exposure to an AI assistant causes 'cognitive spillover' that implicitly shapes subsequent human-human collaboration by altering shared language, collective attention, and social cohesion, even when the AI is no longer present.
result.qual_notes: This paper uses two lab experiments (N=97 individuals, N=69 in 20 teams) to study the mechanisms of AI's influence on teamwork, rather than final performance outcomes. Study 1 used ChatGPT 4.0 in a customer service task, while Study 2 used a non-generative, scripted AI in a problem-solving task. The key finding is that AI's influence is not contained to the direct human-AI interaction but 'spills over' to shape how human team members talk, think, and relate to each other, functioning as a 'social forcefield' that can strengthen or weaken team cohesion depending on context.

paper_metadata.title: When GenAI increases inequality: evidence from a university debating competition
paper_metadata.sample: 142 undergraduate students in a university debating competition

productivity.finding: GenAI provides a small and statistically insignificant overall productivity boost (2.2% higher score) in a complex debating task, with gains being highly dependent on user skill.

inequality.finding: Contrary to many studies, GenAI widens the performance gap between high- and low-ability students in a complex verbal task, with high-ability students benefiting significantly more.

substitute_complement.finding: In tasks requiring higher-order skills like debating, GenAI acts as a complement to human ability, particularly for high-skilled individuals who are better at extracting and utilizing its output.

findings.note: This is a relative increase. The absolute increase was 4.6 percentage points. The effect was not statistically significant.
The effect for low-ability (non-scholarship) students was insignificant, while the effect for high-ability (scholarship) students was significant.
Low-performing students saw a decrease in performance with AI, while high-performing students saw an increase.

findings.note: This was the only debating sub-task where low-ability students showed significant improvement.

findings.metric_definition: Regression coefficient for treatment on the 'Refutation' sub-score for students with a merit scholarship.

result.one_line: In a university debating competition, GenAI widened the performance gap by significantly boosting the productivity of high-ability students while providing no benefit to their low-ability peers.
result.qual_notes: This study was a randomized controlled trial with 142 undergraduate students participating in a debating competition. The task was designed to require higher-order cognitive skills and unpredictable verbal interactions, a context where the authors argue GenAI acts as a complement to skill. The main finding that AI increases inequality contrasts with other studies focusing on more routine or written tasks. The mechanism appears to be that high-ability students are more effective at extracting and using information from the AI.

The coefficient estimate represents a significant improvement by 11.8% for students using ChatGPT among those on a scholarship, and a 1.5% (insignificant) effect of ChatGPT among those without a scholarship.

paper_metadata.authors: Song, F., Agarwal, A., & Wen, W.
paper_metadata.title: The Impact of Generative AI on Collaborative Open-Source Software Development: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
paper_metadata.sample: 7,637 open-source software projects on GitHub

productivity.finding: GitHub Copilot increases project-level code contributions by 5.9% and individual developer productivity by 2.1%, but also increases coordination time by 8%, with a net positive effect on the timely merging of code.

inequality.finding: Core developers benefit more from Copilot than peripheral developers, experiencing larger increases in code contributions and a relatively smaller increase in coordination time, widening the performance gap.

entry_level.finding: AI assistance benefits core developers more than peripheral (less-familiar) developers, who experience smaller productivity gains and face a disproportionately larger increase in coordination time, potentially making it harder for newcomers to contribute effectively.

substitute_complement.finding: AI acts as a complement to human developers, increasing both the number of participating developers (+3.4%) and individual productivity (+2.1%), while also reallocating effort towards increased coordination and code discussion.

findings.comparison: Projects with Copilot adoption vs. synthetic control
findings.metric_definition: The average time taken (in hours) to accept a code submission.
findings.note: This is a negative productivity effect, representing a cost that accompanies the output gains. Value is from the text interpreting the log-level coefficient from Table 2.

findings.note: This represents the effect on the extensive margin of labor.

findings.comparison: Projects with Copilot adoption vs. synthetic control
findings.metric_definition: The average number of merged PRs per developer.
findings.note: This represents the effect on the intensive margin of labor.

findings.comparison: Core developers with Copilot adoption vs. synthetic control
findings.metric_definition: The number of merged PRs submitted by core developers.
findings.note: From an analysis of absolute changes for core developers in Appendix K.

findings.metric: Project-level code contributions (merged PRs) by peripheral developers
findings.comparison: Peripheral developers with Copilot adoption vs. synthetic control
findings.metric_definition: The number of merged PRs submitted by peripheral developers.
findings.note: From an analysis of absolute changes for peripheral developers in Appendix K. The gain is smaller than for core developers (6.1%).

findings.metric_definition: The ratio of the average time taken to accept a code submission from peripheral developers to the average time taken to accept a code submission from all developers.
findings.note: This positive coefficient indicates a disproportionate increase in coordination time for peripheral developers, who are less familiar with projects.

result.one_line: In open-source software development, GitHub Copilot increases code contributions by 5.9% but also raises coordination time by 8%, with core developers benefiting more than peripheral developers.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a generalized synthetic control method on observational data from 7,637 GitHub projects between 2021 and 2022 to estimate the causal impact of Copilot adoption. The productivity gains from increased code contributions are offset by a significant increase in coordination time, driven by more extensive code discussions. The study also finds that code quality (normalized for volume) does not significantly change. The benefits are unequal: core developers see larger productivity gains while peripheral developers, who are less familiar with projects, face a disproportionately larger increase in coordination time, widening the performance gap.

paper_metadata.title: Generative AI's Impact on Graduate Student Writing Productivity and Quality
paper_metadata.sample: 27 graduate students at Carnegie Mellon University

productivity.finding: Generative AI assistance reduced writing time by 64.5% and improved writing quality from a B+ to an A for graduate students on professional writing tasks.

inequality.finding: Generative AI provides slightly more pronounced benefits for ESL students compared to native English speakers, suggesting a leveling effect that compresses performance gaps in both time and quality.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI is used as a complement to human writers, acting as an assistant for sub-tasks like summarization, research, and analysis.

result.one_line: In a study of graduate students, generative AI assistance reduced writing time by 64.5% and improved quality from a B+ to an A, with a more pronounced (though not statistically significant) benefit for ESL students, suggesting a skill-leveling effect.
result.qual_notes: This study used a quasi-experimental, within-subjects design with 27 graduate students at Carnegie Mellon University. Students completed a professional writing task without AI, then received instruction on AI, and then completed a similar task with AI assistance. The study finds large productivity and quality gains, and a non-statistically significant trend towards a leveling effect, where ESL students benefited more than native English speakers. Key limitations include the small sample size, lack of counterbalancing for the order of conditions, and the fact that the grader was not blind to the condition.

evidence.snippet: On average, genAI assistance reduced the time spent completing their policy memo assignment by 64.5%.
On average, students' grades change from a B+ (M= 88.3%, SD = 10.3%) without genAI to an A (M = 94.1%, SD = 8.0%) with genAI assistance.

In productivity, ESL students spent on average 212.36 minutes (SD = 116.75 min) preparing their first assignment without genAI, while native English speaking students spent on average 165.64 minutes (SD = 147.32 min). With genAI assistance, ESL students closed the time to completion gap, averaging only 10 minutes longer to complete the assignment compared to native English speakers, down from an average 47 minute gap on the first assignment.

There was no interaction between writing condition and English language status on writing time (f(1,23) = .45, p = .51, ηp² = .02) meaning, statistically, the amount of time saved writing with genAI assistance did not differ

The observed data show ESL students had an 85% average grade on the no-genAI assignment compared to 87% for native English speaking students. In the final assignment with genAI assistance, cohorts earned equivalent grades (MNative = 94.4%, MESL = 93.9%).

There was no interaction of writing condition and English language status (f(1,25) = .12, p = .74, ηp² = .01) meaning the amount of change in assignment grades did not differ between native English speakers and ESL students (see Figure 3).

Follow-up pairwise testing found that coming into the course, ESL students self-evaluated their English writing competency significantly lower than did native English speakers (t(25) = 3.49, p = .002, d = 1.35). By the end of the semester, this difference had disappeared (t(25) = 1.07, p = .30, d = .41) with ESL students reporting equivalent perceived writing competency to their native English speaking peers.

paper_metadata.title: When combinations of humans and AI are useful: A systematic review and meta-analysis
paper_metadata.sample: Meta-analysis of 106 experimental studies reporting 370 effect sizes.

productivity.finding: On average, human-AI combinations perform worse than the best of either humans or AI alone (Hedges' g = -0.23), with significant losses on decision-making tasks but small, insignificant gains on content creation tasks.

inequality.finding: Human-AI synergy is positive when humans initially outperform the AI, but negative when the AI is superior, suggesting that lower-performing humans (relative to the AI) are not effectively augmented to achieve synergy.

substitute_complement.finding: AI on average complements human performance, as human-AI teams significantly outperform humans alone (Hedges' g = 0.64), but the combined team is often a poor substitute for the best individual performer (human or AI).

result.one_line: A meta-analysis of 106 experiments finds that while AI on average augments human performance, human-AI teams are often worse than the best of either the human or AI working alone, especially in decision-making tasks.
result.qual_notes: This paper is a meta-analysis of 370 effect sizes from 106 experimental studies comparing the performance of humans alone, AI alone, and human-AI combinations. The study finds that human-AI 'synergy' (outperforming the best of either agent) is negative on average, driven by losses in decision-making tasks, but positive (though not statistically significant) in content creation tasks. Conversely, 'human augmentation' (outperforming the human alone) is positive and large on average. Synergy is positive when humans are initially better than the AI, but negative when the AI is better, suggesting humans struggle to correctly utilize a superior AI partner.

evidence.snippet: First, we found that, on average, human-AI combinations performed significantly worse than the best of humans or AI alone (Hedges' g = −0.23; 95% confidence interval, −0.39 to −0.07).
The overall pooled effect was negative (g = −0.23; t92 = −2.89; two-tailed P = 0.005; 95% confidence interval (CI), −0.39 to −0.07) and considered small according to conventional interpretations 21.

The human-AI systems performed significantly better than humans alone, and this pooled effect size was positive (g = 0.64; t98 = 11.87; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.53 to 0.74) and medium to large 21.

Among decision tasks—those in which participants decided between a finite set of options—the pooled effect size for human-AI synergy was significantly negative (g = −0.27; t104 = −3.20; two-tailed P = 0.002; 95% CI, −0.44 to −0.10), which indicates performance losses from combining humans and AI.

In contrast, among creation tasks—those in which participants created some sort of open-response content—the pooled effect size for human-AI synergy was positive (g = 0.19; t104 = 1.35; two-tailed P = 0.180; 95% CI, −0.09 to 0.48), pointing to synergy between humans and AI.

But when the AI alone outperformed the human alone, performance losses occurred in the combined system relative to the AI alone, with a negative effect size for human-AI synergy of g = −0.54 (t104 = −6.20; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, −0.71 to −0.37), a medium-sized effect21.

As shown in Fig. 2, when the human alone outperformed the AI alone, the combined human-AI system outperformed both alone with an average pooled effect size for human-AI synergy of g = 0.46 (t104 = 5.06; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.28 to 0.66), a medium-sized effect21.

When the AI outperformed the human alone, greater performance gains tended to occur in the human-AI systems relative to the human alone, and the pooled effect size for human augmentation was positive and medium to large in magnitude (g = 0.74; t104 = 13.50; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.63 to 0.85)
Here we addressed this question by conducting a preregistered systematic review and meta-analysis of 106 experimental studies reporting 370 effect sizes.

result.one_line: In a large field experiment with over 31,000 scientific preprints, providing LLM-generated feedback increased manuscript revision rates by 12.55%, with the largest gains for researchers who are non-native English speakers, less established, or at earlier career stages.
result.qual_notes: This study was a large-scale randomized field experiment where authors of arXiv preprints were randomly assigned to receive customized LLM-generated feedback on their manuscript. The primary outcome was the rate of subsequent revision. The study finds that AI feedback acts as a complement, increasing revision activity, and serves as an 'equalizer' by disproportionately benefiting researchers with less access to traditional feedback networks (e.g., non-English speakers, early-career researchers). A key caveat is that the study measures an intent-to-treat effect, which is a lower bound on the effect for authors who actually engaged with the feedback.

evidence.snippet: Treated papers exhibited an average increase of 0.005 revisions within one month relative to the control group (p < 0.05), corresponding to a 12.55% increase over the baseline revision rate.
Among manuscripts affiliated exclusively with non-English-dominant environments, AI feedback increases revisions by 0.007 (P < 0.05) corresponding to a 19.9% relative increase over control baseline.
A comparable effect is observed among manuscripts with lower embeddedness (0.007; P < 0.05), corresponding to a 26.4% increase relative to the control group.
At the author level, statistically significant effects are likewise concentrated among authors with lower h-index (20.2% increase; P < 0.05)
...and earlier career age (18.9% increase; P < 0.05).

In contrast, the corresponding estimates for manuscripts and authors in the higher category (i.e., English-dominant environments, higher scholarly embeddedness, higher h-index, and longer career age) are small and statistically indistinguishable from zero ( P = 0.98, 0.63, 0.31, and 0.28).

We find that, among authors whose pre-treatment writing showed minimal detectable LLM involvement, treated authors exhibit significantly higher subsequent LLM adoption than controls (Atreatment = 0.150, Acontrol = 0.142; Fig. 3A), corresponding to a 5.29% relative increase in adoption intensity within twelve months ( P < 0.05; SM Table S7).

result.one_line: Using a large dataset of US worker profiles, this study finds that firm-level exposure to generative AI since ChatGPT's release caused starting wages to fall by 4.5%, with larger declines for junior (-6.3%) and mid-level (-5.9%) workers and a shift in hiring away from junior roles.
result.qual_notes: This quasi-experimental study uses a difference-in-differences design comparing US firms with high vs. low pre-existing occupational exposure to LLMs, following the release of ChatGPT in November 2022. The analysis is based on a large dataset of 138 million worker profiles from Revelio Technologies, which includes imputed wage data. The paper finds that AI exposure reduces wages and hiring for junior and mid-level workers while leaving senior workers unaffected, and shifts hiring from junior to mid-level roles. Effects are largest in the Information sector (-13% wage decline).

evidence.snippet: Using a large-scale dataset of realized worker-firm matches for 138 million U.S. workers, we document that the arrival of ChatGPT significantly reduced the wages of firms whose ex-ante occupation structure was more exposed to automation relative to less exposed firms.

Using a large-scale dataset of realized worker-firm matches for 138 million U.S. workers, we document that the arrival of ChatGPT significantly reduced the wages of firms whose ex-ante occupation structure was more exposed to automation relative to less exposed firms.
In particular, we find that the average wage of exposed firms fell 4.5% relative to unexposed firms.

We also find that starting wages for new positions for junior and mid-level workers in exposed firms fell by 6.3% and 5.9% respectively relative to unexposed firms, while senior wages remained stable or even increased.

result.one_line: In a field RCT with experienced open-source developers, using generative AI tools increased task completion time by 19%, contradicting both developer and expert expectations of a speedup.
result.qual_notes: This study (N=16 developers, 246 tasks) finds a surprising productivity decrease from using early-2025 AI tools (primarily Cursor Pro with Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet) on real tasks in mature, familiar open-source projects. The slowdown was larger for tasks where developers had high prior experience, suggesting AI is less helpful for experts on familiar work. AI usage shifted developers' time away from coding and searching towards prompting, waiting for, and reviewing AI outputs. The authors suggest the slowdown may be specific to their setting of experienced developers working in large, complex codebases they are already familiar with.

evidence.snippet: Surprisingly, we find that allowing AI actually increases completion time by 19%-AI tooling slowed developers down.

Figure 1: Experts and study participants (experienced open-source contributors) substantially over- estimate how much AI assistance will speed up developers-tasks take 19% more time when study participants can use AI tools like Cursor Pro.
Before starting tasks, developers forecast that allowing AI will reduce completion time by 24%.
After completing the study, developers estimate that allowing AI reduced completion time by 20%.
This slowdown also contradicts predictions from experts in economics (39% shorter)
and ML (38% shorter).

When allowed to use AI, developers spend a smaller proportion of their time actively coding and reading/searching for information. Instead, they spend time reviewing AI outputs, prompting AI systems, and waiting for AI generations.

result.one_line: Using large-scale payroll data, this study finds that generative AI has led to a 16% relative decline in employment for early-career workers (ages 22-25) in exposed occupations, particularly where AI automates rather than augments tasks.
result.qual_notes: This study uses high-frequency administrative payroll data from ADP for millions of US workers from 2021-2025. The main finding comes from a quasi-experimental event study design controlling for firm-level shocks, showing a robust negative employment effect on young workers in AI-exposed jobs. The effect is concentrated in occupations where AI serves as a substitute (automation) rather than a complement (augmentation), and adjustments occur through employment quantity rather than wages, which appear sticky.

evidence.snippet: Early-career workers (ages 22-25) in AI-exposed occupations experienced 16% relative employment declines, controlling for firm-level shocks, while employment for experienced workers remained stable.
After conditioning on firm-time effects, young workers experienced a 16% relative employment decline in the most exposed occupations.
In contrast, for the highest two exposure quintiles employment for 22-25 year olds declined by 6% between late 2022 and September 2025, while employment for workers aged 35-49 grew by over 8%.
By September 2025, employment for software developers aged 22-25 declined nearly 20% compared to its peak in late 2022.

result.one_line: A generative AI assistant for customer service agents increased productivity by 15%, with the largest gains (36%) for the lowest-skilled workers, effectively compressing the performance gap between novices and experienced employees.
result.qual_notes: The study uses a staggered rollout of a GPT-3-based conversational assistant to 5,172 customer support agents at a Fortune 500 company. The AI disproportionately benefited less-skilled and less-experienced workers, raising their performance to the level of more senior colleagues and reducing attrition among new hires. While low-skill workers saw large gains, the most-skilled workers experienced no productivity boost and a small decline in the quality of their work. The productivity gains appear to stem from durable learning, as agents who used the AI performed better even during system outages.

evidence.snippet: As we add controls, our effects fall slightly, so that with agent and tenure fixed effects, we find that the AI deployment increases RPH by 0.30 chats or 15.2%.
Post AI × Ever treated 0.301*** (0.0329)

In Table III, we report additional results using our preferred specification with fixed effects for year-month, agent, location, and agent tenure. Column (1) documents a 3.7-minute decrease in the average duration of customer chats, an 8.5% decline from the baseline mean of 43 minutes (shorter handle times are considered better).
Column (2) indicates a 0.37 unit increase in the number of chats that an agent can handle per hour. Relative to a baseline mean of 2.4, this represents an increase of roughly 15%.

We find that the productivity effect of AI assistance is most pronounced for workers in the lowest skill quintile (leftmost side), who see an increase of 0.5 in RPH, or 36%.
In contrast, AI assistance does not lead to any significant change in productivity for the most skilled workers (rightmost side).

In Figure IV, Panel A, we see a clear monotonic pattern in which the least experienced agents see the greatest gains in RPH. Agents with less than one month of tenure improve by 0.7 RPH, with larger effects for less experienced workers. In contrast, we see no effect for agents with more than a year of tenure.

Consistent with our findings so far, Online Appendix Figure A.XVIII, Panel A shows that access to AI assistance is associated with the strongest reductions in attrition among newer agents, those with less than six months of experience. The magnitude of this coefficient, around 10 percentage points, translates into a 40% decrease relative to a baseline attrition rate in this group of 25%.
Relative to a baseline rate of approximately 6%, these coefficients suggest that AI assistance generates an almost 25% decline in customer requests to speak to a manager.
We study the staggered introduction of a generative AI-based conversational assistant using data from 5,172 customer-support agents.
The tool we study is built on Generative Pre-trained Transformer 3 (GPT-3), a member of the GPT family of large language models developed by OpenAI (OpenAI 2023).

Less experienced and lower-skilled workers improve both the speed and quality of their output, while the most experienced and highest-skilled workers see small gains in speed and small declines in quality.

The AI tool also helps newer agents to move more quickly down the experience curve: treated agents with two months of tenure perform just as well as untreated agents with more than six months of tenure.
Importantly, the AI system we study is designed to augment (rather than replace) human agents.
Access to AI assistance increases worker productivity, as measured by issues resolved per hour, by 15% on average, with substantial heterogeneity across workers.

result.one_line: In three field experiments with 4,867 software developers, access to a generative AI coding assistant increased the number of completed tasks by 26%, with larger, though not always statistically significant, gains for less experienced and junior developers.
result.qual_notes: This study analyzes data from three separate randomized controlled trials at Microsoft, Accenture, and an anonymous Fortune 100 company, where developers were given access to GitHub Copilot. The main estimates are from a weighted instrumental variable (IV) regression to account for imperfect adoption, representing a local average treatment effect (LATE) for adopters. While the paper finds that less experienced developers benefit more, these heterogeneity results are often not statistically significant. The study focuses on productivity and quality metrics, not labor market outcomes like hiring or substitution.

result.qual_notes: This field RCT at Procter & Gamble used a 2x2 design (individual/team vs. with/without AI) on a new product ideation task. productivity.finding: Generative AI users saved two hours per week on email and reduced their work outside of regular hours, but did not change the quantity or composition of their tasks.

inequality.finding: The study finds no evidence of heterogeneous treatment effects, suggesting that time savings from AI were similar across different types of workers.

substitute_complement.finding: AI primarily complemented workers by helping them complete existing tasks more quickly (saving time on email), without substituting for labor or leading to significant changes in task composition or team coordination.

result.one_line: In a large field experiment, providing knowledge workers with generative AI saved them two hours of email time per week and reduced after-hours work, but did not change the quantity or composition of their tasks.
result.qual_notes: This field experiment randomized access to Microsoft 365 Copilot for 7,137 knowledge workers across 66 large firms over six months. The study uses telemetry data to measure changes in time use and task quantities, finding that AI users saved 17% of their time on email without reducing the number of threads they replied to. However, the study found no significant changes in time spent on meetings or document writing, nor in the quantity of outputs in those domains, suggesting AI primarily complemented existing work patterns rather than substituting tasks or reorganizing work. A key limitation is the lack of direct productivity or quality measures; effects are inferred from time use and activity counts.

evidence.snippet: Our intent-to-treat (LATE) estimates show workers with access to Copilot spent an average of 1.4 (2) fewer hours per week in email sessions over post-period months 4-6, a 12% (17%) decline from their pre-period average.
We present evidence from a field experiment across 66 firms and 7,137 knowledge workers.
In the second half of the 6-month experiment, the 80% of treated workers who used this tool spent two fewer hours on email each week and reduced their time working outside of regular hours.

We cannot reject the null of 0 treatment effect heterogeneity for total Outlook session time when we allow the model to choose which dimensions of potential heterogeneity matter via elastic net or a neural networks approach
Apart from these individual time savings, we do not detect shifts in the quantity or composition of workers' tasks resulting from individual-level AI provision.

substitute_complement.finding: Higher AI exposure is associated with reduced employment and work hours in occupations intensive in cognitive analytical tasks, but with employment gains in occupations intensive in manual physical tasks, suggesting AI substitutes for the former and may complement the latter.

inequality.finding: Adverse labor market effects from AI exposure are more pronounced for younger (<30) and older (>50) workers compared to prime-age workers, and for men compared to women; more educated workers face smaller employment declines but larger shifts in work intensity.

entry_level.finding: Workers under 30 experience larger employment declines in response to rising AI exposure compared to prime-age (30-50) workers, suggesting AI may be negatively impacting the entry-level labor market.

result.one_line: From 2022-2025, US occupations with higher generative AI exposure experienced significant declines in employment and hours worked and increases in unemployment, with the most adverse effects on younger, older, and male workers in analytical roles.
result.qual_notes: This study constructs a dynamic measure of occupational AI exposure using LLMs (ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.5) to rate tasks from O*NET. It links this exposure score to US labor market outcomes from the Current Population Survey (CPS) from 2022 to 2025, using a first-differenced regression model to estimate associations between changes in AI exposure and labor outcomes. The authors caution that the results are associations, not causal effects, and that the AI exposure score measures potential capability, not actual adoption. The study finds that negative labor market effects accelerated in the later period (Oct 2023-Mar 2025), and that occupations with the largest increases in AI exposure experienced significantly larger employment losses.

evidence.snippet: In the fully specified model, a 10 point increase in exposure is associated with a 5.6 percentage point decline in total occupational employment (s.e. 2.0 ppt) and a 0.64 percentage point increase in unemployment (s.e. 0.26 ppt) when using ChatGPT-generated scores
▲ ChatGPT Exp. -0.558 (0.202)
and with an 8.5 percentage point decline in employment (s.e. 1.9 ppt) and a 0.68 percentage point increase in unemployment (s.e. 0.27) when using Claude-generated scores.

Using Claude- generated exposure scores, a 10-point increase in exposure is associated with a 6.19 percent- age point decline in employment for workers aged 30–50, compared to a 7.67 percentage point decline for workers under 30 and a 6.66 percentage point decline for those over 50.

For example, using ChatGPT-generated scores, a 10-point increase in exposure is associated with a 9.3 percentage point decline in total employment for workers without a bachelor’s degree, compared to a 7.5 percentage point decline for those with a bachelor’s degree or more.

A 10 point increase in AI exposure is associated with employment gains of 10.1 percentage points for top-quartile occupations using ChatGPT- based exposure scores, compared to a 6.8 point decline (s.e. 2.0 ppt) for non-top-quartile occupations, implying a relatively precisely estimated differential effect of +16.9 percentage points (s.e. 5.5 ppt).

productivity.finding: On a document comprehension task, generative AI increased quality by 17% and reduced completion time by 34%, but on a data analysis task, it reduced quality by 12% with no significant change in time.

inequality.finding: On a document comprehension task, generative AI compressed the performance gap, with the largest performance gains observed among participants in the bottom half of baseline skill.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI was used as a complement to human work, with 50% of users combining the AI's output with their own knowledge rather than using it verbatim, a style the authors describe as "Cyborg" interaction.

result.one_line: A field experiment with public sector knowledge workers finds that generative AI improves performance (17% higher quality, 34% faster) on a document comprehension task, especially for lower-skilled workers, but harms performance (12% lower quality) on a data analysis task.
result.qual_notes: The study is a randomized controlled trial with 143 staff at the Central Bank of Ireland, assigned to either a document comprehension or data analysis task. The productivity effects of AI were highly task-dependent, being positive for the document task and negative for the data task. For the document task, AI compressed the performance gap, with lower-skilled workers benefiting most. The paper notes that most users did not use the AI output verbatim, but combined it with their own knowledge, suggesting a complementary role.

evidence.snippet: For the Documents task, the treatment group using Gen AI had a 17% improvement in answer quality scores (as judged by human evaluators) and a 34% improvement in task completion time compared to a control group.

The average quality score for the treatment group was 2.21 compared to 1.89 in the control group, a mean difference of 0.32. This represents a 17% improvement in quality for the treatment group. A two-tailed t-test confirmed that this difference was significant (p < 0.05).

On average, participants in the treatment group completed the task in 33 minutes, which was significantly shorter than the control group's average completion time of 50 minutes. A mean difference of 17 minutes represents a 34% improvement for the treatment group in average time to complete the task compared to the control group. The time difference was statistically significant (p < 0.05), suggesting the use of Gen AI reduced the time participants spent in answering the comprehension questions.
For the Data task, we find the Gen AI treatment group experienced a 12% reduction in quality scores and no significant difference in mean completion time compared to the control group.
This equates to a 12% reduction in quality for the treatment group. A two-tailed t-test confirmed that this difference is significant (p < 0.005).

The results are visualised in the density plot (Figure 5) and show that the average time for participants in the control group (32.5 minutes) was marginally lower when compared to the treatment group (33.5 minutes). This represents a 3% increase in timing for the treatment group over the control group. However a two-tailed t-test confirms that this difference is not statistically significant (p-value = 0.7048).
The average quality score for bottom-half participants that used Gen AI increased from 1.38 on the assessment task to 2.12 on the experiment task (see Figure 6).

Bottom Half Assessment Performers - Scores
[Chart showing Treatment group scores of 1.38 (Assessment) and 2.12 (Experiment), and Control group scores of 1.61 (Assessment) and 1.75 (Experiment)]

For users in the top 50% of baseline scores, Gen AI use on the experiment task led to a decrease in average quality score from 2.67 on the assessment task to 2.28 on the experiment task (see Figure 8).

Top Half Assessment Performers - Scores
[Chart showing Treatment group scores of 2.67 (Assessment) and 2.28 (Experiment), and Control group scores of 2.72 (Assessment) and 2.05 (Experiment)]
This decline was less than that observed in the control group whose scores decreased from 2.72 on the assessment task to 2.05 on the assessment task.
This involved two representative tasks a document comprehension and data analytics task among 143 staff, representing about 7% of the staff population of the CBI.
The analysis revealed that the most significant gains from using Gen AI were observed among participants in the lower 50% of assessment task scores.

In terms of outputs, just 33% of respondents took the responses directly from the Gen AI application as their final answer, while 50% used a combination of the Gen AI response and their own knowledge to formulate their answer. This aligns with the “Cyborg" style of human-AI interaction noted by [5], where users fully integrate AI and human capabilities into their work.

paper_metadata.sample: 58 million LinkedIn profiles and 14 million job postings from 2014-2023 for publicly traded US companies in Compustat.

productivity.finding: AI adoption leads to significant increases in firm revenue, profits, TFP, and overall employment, driven by firm-level productivity gains.

inequality.finding: AI exposure is highest for high-wage jobs, but while substitution effects negatively impact them, offsetting firm-growth and task-reallocation effects lead to a modest net increase in their relative employment share.

substitute_complement.finding: AI substitutes for labor in exposed tasks (reducing employment), but this is offset by workers reallocating to complementary tasks (increasing employment) and by firm-level productivity gains that boost labor demand.

result.one_line: AI adoption increases firm productivity and overall employment, but within firms, it substitutes for labor in highly exposed tasks, particularly in high-wage occupations, though these negative effects are muted by workers reallocating to complementary tasks.
result.qual_notes: The study uses a novel dataset of AI applications derived from 58 million resumes from 2014-2023, linked to job postings and firm data for publicly traded US companies. It employs an instrumental variable strategy based on firms' historical university hiring networks to identify causal effects. The analysis largely predates the rise of generative AI. The main finding is that while AI is a strong substitute for specific tasks (especially in high-wage jobs), this is counteracted by within-occupation task reallocation and firm-level productivity gains, leading to modest net employment effects.

evidence.snippet: Task-level AI Exposure -1.91*** (-19.21)

AI Exposure Concentration 7.46*** (5.38)

At the top of the earnings distribution, our estimates imply that these occupations lose about 7% of employment share over five years from this direct effect, while lower-paid occupations gain nearly 10% in relative terms.
At the very top, the net effect is positive at around 3 percent over a 5-year period, despite substantial direct exposure for these occupations.

The direct substitution effect is again strongest for high-paying jobs: at the 90th percentile, within-firm employment falls by about 2.8% over five years. Concentration of exposure across tasks tempers this decline to about 1.5%.
At the other end of the distribution, low-wage jobs see modest employment gains of roughly 2%.
Our measure of AI adoption ends in 2023 and therefore largely excludes the recent rise of generative AI (GenAI).

After applying these restrictions, our dataset includes approximately 58 million LinkedIn profiles and 14 million job postings that are linked to publicly traded companies in Compustat which includes data on firm outcomes.

result.one_line: In an experiment with UK workers, generative AI boosted productivity on text-based tasks but did not reduce performance inequality between demographic groups, challenging the narrative of AI as a 'great equalizer'.
result.qual_notes: This study was a pre-registered online experiment with 1,041 working-age UK residents who already had a ChatGPT account. Participants were randomly assigned to use or not use ChatGPT for three text-based tasks of varying complexity and ambiguity. A key caveat is that the sample is not representative of the general population, as it is pre-selected for AI familiarity. The study finds that productivity gains depend not just on task complexity but also ambiguity, and that more frequent AI users (a proxy for skill) benefit more on ambiguous tasks, suggesting skill complementarity.

paper_metadata.title: STILL WATERS, RAPID CURRENTS: EARLY LABOR MARKET TRANSFORMATION UNDER GENERATIVE AI
paper_metadata.sample: 25,000 workers across 7,000 workplaces in 11 highly exposed occupations in Denmark

productivity.finding: Despite workers reporting productivity benefits from AI, there are precise null effects on their earnings and hours worked two years after ChatGPT's launch, with confidence intervals ruling out average effects larger than 2%.

inequality.finding: Generative AI is associated with increased occupational mobility, particularly for adopters who move into higher-paying occupations, suggesting a channel for career progression rather than skill compression within existing roles.

entry_level.finding: While early-career employment has declined in AI-exposed occupations, this trend is not driven by firms that have actually adopted generative AI, with precise estimates ruling out effects on the early-career employment share greater than a third of a percentage point.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI primarily complements labor by reorganizing work and creating new tasks in areas like content generation, AI oversight, and integration, rather than substituting for workers on the margin of total labor input.

result.one_line: Generative AI adoption in Denmark led to significant work reorganization and new task creation but had precisely zero effect on workers' earnings, hours, or the employment of early-career staff in the first two years.
result.qual_notes: This study links large-scale surveys of 25,000 workers in 11 AI-exposed occupations in Denmark to administrative labor market data. Using a difference-in-differences design, it finds that while workers report productivity gains and firms are reorganizing work to incorporate AI, these 'rapid currents' have not yet translated into changes in 'still water' outcomes like earnings or employment. The main observable effect is increased occupational mobility for AI adopters, who tend to move into higher-paying roles. The null effects on earnings and employment are precise, ruling out changes larger than 2% for workers or firms.

paper_metadata.title: From helping hand to stumbling block: the ChatGPT paradox in competency experiment
paper_metadata.sample: 349 Korean-speaking university students

productivity.finding: ChatGPT assistance significantly decreased performance on mathematical problem-solving tasks, while having no significant average effect on reading/writing or computational thinking tasks.

inequality.finding: ChatGPT harmed the performance of low-ability participants in a math task because they could not discern incorrect AI answers, while for a writing task, it benefited intermediate-ability participants but not low- or high-ability ones.

substitute_complement.finding: The findings contest the idea that ChatGPT complements workers with less expertise, showing it can be a harmful substitute for low-skilled workers in tasks where they cannot validate the AI's output.

findings.metric_definition: p-value from a t-test comparing the mean scores of the treatment (ChatGPT) and control groups on a mathematical problem-solving task.
findings.note: The paper reports a significant decrease in performance but does not state the magnitude of the effect (e.g., mean scores or percentage change).

findings.metric: p-value for difference in mean scores
findings.comparison: ChatGPT vs Control (Reading and writing task)
findings.metric_definition: p-value from a t-test comparing the mean scores of the treatment (ChatGPT) and control groups on a reading and writing task.
findings.note: The paper reports no significant difference in the average scores between groups.

findings.comparison: ChatGPT vs Control (Reading and writing task)
findings.metric_definition: p-value from a Wilcoxon rank-sum test comparing the score distributions of the treatment (ChatGPT) and control groups on a reading and writing task.
findings.note: While average scores did not differ, the distributions did, suggesting intermediate-performers benefited while low and high-performers did not.

findings.metric: p-value for difference in mean scores
findings.comparison: ChatGPT vs Control (Computational thinking task)

findings.note: The paper reports no significant difference in the average scores between groups.

result.one_line: In a lab experiment with students, ChatGPT assistance significantly decreased performance on mathematical tasks, particularly for low-ability participants who could not discern incorrect AI answers, while having no average effect on writing or computational thinking tasks.
result.qual_notes: This lab experiment randomized 349 university students into using ChatGPT or a search engine for reading/writing, mathematical, and computational thinking tasks. The key finding is a significant performance decrease on the math task, attributed to low-ability users trusting plausible but incorrect AI-generated answers. For writing tasks, there was no average effect, but a distributional shift suggested benefits for intermediate-performers. The study highlights the risk of AI for users who lack the domain expertise to validate its output, challenging the idea that AI primarily complements low-skilled workers.

evidence.snippet: For the M-task, the average score of the treatment group is also significantly lower than the control group (p-value = 0.002).
In the W-task, the null hypothesis that the average score of the treatment group is the same as the control group cannot be rejected (t-test, p-value = 0.138)
but the null hypothesis that the score distribution of the control group and the treatment group is the same is rejected at the 5% significance level (Wilcoxon rank-sum test, p-value = 0.0489).

Regarding the C-task, no significant difference was found between the control and treatment groups. The null hypothesis on the draws from the same population is not rejected (p-value = 0.482), and the mean score difference is statistically insignificant (p-value = 0.544).

We ran a controlled laboratory experiment to examine whether ChatGPT's aid can increase the participants' performance in three different – reading and writing, mathematical problem-solving, and computational thinking – tasks.
This section reports the results of our experiment, which involved a total of 349 participants, 181 treated, focusing on how much the task scores improved with ChatGPT's assistance.
The participants, Korean-speaking university students aged 19 or older with diverse academic disciplines, were randomly divided into one of two groups

This result is mainly because the low-ability subjects couldn't discern the hallucinated answers with the correct ones, and it contests the general idea that ChatGPT can complement the workers with less expertise.

paper_metadata.title: Human-AI Teams' Impact on Organizations – A Review
paper_metadata.sample: A systematic literature review of 122 academic papers on human-AI teams.

productivity.finding: The reviewed literature suggests that a key use case for human-AI teams is to increase work efficiency and decrease employee workloads, thereby improving performance and productivity.

entry_level.finding: The paper suggests that as AI takes over routine data analysis tasks, there will be a change in human role structure and potentially a reduction in the number of human junior analyst roles.

substitute_complement.finding: The reviewed literature indicates AI both complements human labor by taking over routine tasks to free humans for more strategic work, and substitutes for it, with the potential for headcount reduction and the disestablishment of some existing roles.

result.one_line: In a systematic review of 122 papers, the authors find that human-AI teams are expected to increase productivity by automating routine tasks, but this may lead to a reduction in junior-level roles and the disestablishment of other positions as work is restructured.
result.qual_notes: This paper is a systematic literature review of 122 articles on human-AI teams (HAITs) in white-collar organizations. It synthesizes findings on key success factors, use cases, and challenges. The paper theorizes about impacts on organizational structure but does not present new empirical data or synthesize quantitative results from the reviewed literature, focusing instead on qualitative themes like job insecurity and the need for new skills.
