As Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) is encroaching further on our work and social environments, we examine the increasingly common situation where teams of white-collar workers include both humans and AI. We explore three key themes via a comprehensive (n=122) systematic literature review: key success factors, use cases for human-AI teams, and challenges faced when implementing human-Al teams.
The common theme throughout the identified use cases is that they are predominantly implemented to increase work efficiency and decrease employee workloads (Li, et al., 2023).

There will be potential for headcount reduction or expansion of business scope.

result.one_line: A field experiment with central bank employees found generative AI increased task quality by 50% and reduced completion time by 21%, with larger gains for non-routine tasks and for lower-skilled workers on generalist tasks.
result.qual_notes: The study was a field experiment (RCT) with 101 employees at the National Bank of Slovakia, who completed a series of generalist and specialist tasks with or without access to GPT-4o. The paper finds strong evidence of complementarity between AI and non-routine tasks, where quality gains were more than double those for routine tasks (58% vs 24%). For generalist tasks, AI had an equalizing effect, compressing the performance gap by providing larger quality gains to lower-skilled workers, though this pattern did not hold for specialist tasks or for efficiency gains. A simulation suggests that reallocating workers to tasks based on their AI-augmented comparative advantage could increase organizational output by 7.3%.

result.one_line: A re-analysis of five brainstorming experiments finds that while generative AI may improve the quality of individual ideas, it significantly reduces the overall diversity of the idea pool.
result.qual_notes: This paper is a 'Matters Arising' comment that re-analyzes publicly available data from five experiments originally published by Lee and Chung (2024). The authors measure idea diversity by calculating the fraction of 'unique' ideas, defined as those with low semantic similarity (cosine similarity < 0.8) to other ideas in the set. paper_metadata.title: Generative AI in Action: Field Experimental Evidence from Alibaba's Customer Service Operations
paper_metadata.sample: 5,940 new customer service agents (tenure less than one year) at Alibaba in China.

productivity.finding: Generative AI significantly improved customer service agents' speed (reducing issue identification time by 32% and chat duration by 4%) and subjective quality (increasing ratings and lowering dissatisfaction), but had no effect on objective quality (customer retrial rates).

inequality.finding: Generative AI narrowed the performance gap between agents, significantly boosting the service quality of low-performers while decreasing the quality provided by top-performers.

entry_level.finding: For new customer service agents with less than one year of tenure, generative AI significantly boosts the performance of the lowest-skilled workers, effectively acting as a performance equalizer for early-career employees.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI complements human agents by automating diagnosis and solution proposals, which reshapes their work by enabling more informative communication and, for top performers, by inducing more multitasking between concurrent chats.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with 5,940 new customer service agents at Alibaba, a generative AI assistant increased service speed and subjective quality, disproportionately benefiting low-performers and narrowing skill gaps, while high-performers' quality declined due to increased multitasking.
result.qual_notes: This large-scale field RCT studied new customer service agents (less than one year tenure) at Alibaba. The AI assistant provided diagnostic and solution suggestions. The study finds that while overall productivity (speed, subjective quality) increased, objective quality (retrial rates) did not. The key finding is a performance compression: low-skilled agents improved significantly, while high-skilled agents' quality declined. The paper attributes the decline for high-performers not to over-reliance on AI, but to a shift towards more multitasking, which increased customer wait times and retrials.

evidence.snippet: For service speed, Table 4A Columns (2)–(3) present the second-stage estimates, indicating that 100% gen AI usage decreases issue identification time by 32.3%
and chat duration by 4.2%.
For service quality, Table 4B Columns (2) and (3) show that 100% gen Al usage lowers the dissatisfaction rate by 0.054
and raises average customer ratings by 0.184 points on a five-point scale

Column (2) indicates that at 100% usage rate, Q1 agents shorten chat duration by 14.8% (p < 0.01), Q2 by 5.3% (p > 0.1), and Q3 by 9.0% (p < 0.01), whereas Q4 and Q5 showed no statistically significant changes.
At 100% gen Al usage rate, Q1 agents benefit the most: their dissatisfaction rate decreases by 0.567 (p < 0.01) and their customer ratings increase by 2.144 (p < 0.01).

These effects decline steadily with higher baseline performance, becoming insignificant for Q4 and reversing for Q5, where dissatisfaction rates increase by 0.374 (p < 0.01) and ratings fall by 1.514 (p < 0.01).
For objective quality, measured by the three-day retrial rate, gen Al usage has no statistically significant effects for Q1–Q4 agents (Table 8 Columns (5)).
The only exception is Q5 (i.e., top quintile), where customer retrial rate increases by 0.041 (p < 0.05), again indicating a quality decline among top performers.
At 100% gen AI usage, the shift-away time decreases for Q1–Q4 agents (-31.2%, -14.7%, -15.4%, and -20.5%) but increases by 26.3% for Q5 agents.
Impact on Q5 Agents 0.263***
This large-scale field test involved 5,940 new customer service agents (tenure less than one year), resulting in roughly 2.56 million chats and 0.39 million customer ratings.
Out of the 5,940 agents, 2,895 (48.7%) were randomly assigned to the treatment group and 3,045 (51.3%) were randomly assigned to the control group by their agent ID.

paper_metadata.sample: 78 employees at a large UK fintech firm, comprising 12 web analysts (insiders), 26 marketing specialists (adjacent outsiders), and 40 technology specialists (distant outsiders).

productivity.finding: Generative AI significantly improves quality and dramatically reduces completion time for both structured (conceptualization) and unstructured (execution) content creation tasks.

inequality.finding: GenAI compresses performance gaps between occupational insiders and 'adjacent' outsiders, but a 'GenAI wall' emerges for 'distant' outsiders who lack foundational knowledge, particularly in complex execution tasks.

substitute_complement.finding: GenAI acts as a substitute for insider-specific expertise, enabling workers from adjacent occupations to perform insider tasks without retraining and suggesting a potential for more fluid task allocation across job roles.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with content creators, marketers, and technologists, generative AI helps workers perform tasks from adjacent occupations but fails to bridge the performance gap for workers from distant occupations, especially for complex execution tasks.
result.qual_notes: This study is a field experiment at a UK fintech firm where 78 employees from three occupational groups (web analysts, marketing specialists, technology specialists) were randomly assigned to use a bespoke GenAI tool for article conceptualization and writing. The key finding is the 'GenAI Wall': AI helps 'adjacent outsiders' (marketers) perform as well as 'insiders' (web analysts), but not 'distant outsiders' (technologists), who lack the foundational knowledge to leverage the tool effectively for complex execution tasks. A key limitation is the small sample size, particularly in the control condition for the article execution task, which was underpowered due to non-compliance.

productivity.finding: Human workers paired with an AI teammate under competitive pressure perform 26% worse than all-human teams, an effect that disappears in non-competitive or individual settings.

substitute_complement.finding: AI serves as a poor complement for a human teammate in competitive contexts, as humans exhibit a 'commitment deficit' and reduce their effort when paired with a non-human agent.

result.one_line: In a competitive setting, humans teamed with an AI teammate perform 26% worse than those teamed with a human, a deficit attributed to a lack of social commitment to the AI.
result.qual_notes: The study uses two experiments (N=1,988 online, N=214 lab) with arithmetic and visual counting tasks. The key finding is that performance deficits in human-AI teams only emerge under competitive pressure; in non-competitive or individual settings, performance is equivalent. The authors argue this is due to a "commitment deficit"—a motivational, not technical or trust-based, failure—as participants do not feel the same social obligation to an AI teammate as to a human one. The AI was not generative but was programmed to match human performance levels.

productivity.finding: AI adoption causally increases firm-level labor productivity by 4% on average in European firms, with larger gains for firms that make complementary investments in software, data, and training.

substitute_complement.finding: AI adoption complements labor in the short run, augmenting worker output and increasing labor productivity without reducing firm-level employment, and is associated with higher average wages.

The findings suggest AI acts as a labor-augmenting technology in the short-run, but the productivity gains are uneven, favoring medium and large firms over smaller ones. paper_metadata.title: Generative AI and the Productivity Divide: Human-AI Complementarities in Education and Knowledge Work (Working Paper)
paper_metadata.sample: 179 university students (primarily engineering majors) at Texas A&M, serving as analogs for early-career knowledge workers.

productivity.finding: On average, access to generative AI increased task performance by approximately 17% compared to using traditional resources for self-study.

inequality.finding: Generative AI compressed performance gaps based on prior knowledge but created a new performance divide based on 'AI Interaction Competence' (AIC), with high-AIC individuals benefiting most.

entry_level.finding: In a sample of early-career worker analogs, performance with generative AI depended more on a new skill, 'AI Interaction Competence', than on traditional markers like GPA or prior knowledge.

substitute_complement.finding: The study frames generative AI as a complement to human skill, finding that its effectiveness is determined by a user's 'AI Interaction Competence' in prompting, filtering, and applying outputs.

findings.note: Lower variance among novices who received scaffolding (compared to SD=0.22 for unguided novices) indicates inequality compression.

findings.metric: Standard deviation of exam scores (novices, unguided)
findings.metric_definition: The standard deviation of post-intervention exam scores for novice participants who used the LLM without a conceptual roadmap.
findings.note: Higher variance among unguided novices compared to those who received scaffolding (SD=0.14).

result.one_line: In a lab experiment with early-career worker analogs, generative AI boosted average performance by 17% but also created a new 'productivity divide', where individuals with high 'AI Interaction Competence' (AIC) reaped the largest gains, regardless of their prior knowledge.
result.qual_notes: This study was a randomized controlled trial with 179 university students tasked with self-studying a technical topic (LLMs) using either traditional resources or ChatGPT. The key finding is that while AI access boosts average performance, it creates a new axis of inequality based on 'AI Interaction Competence' (AIC)—the tacit skill of prompting and filtering. A scaffolding intervention (providing a conceptual map) was shown to mitigate this inequality by disproportionately helping low-AIC novices and reducing performance variance.

evidence.snippet: From a managerial standpoint, our results point into a consistent pattern: GenAI access translated into an approximate 17% productivity lift — consistent with field evidence of AI-assisted performance gains in professional work settings.
The average post-intervention exam score among LLM participants was M = .56, SD = .26, compared to M = .48, SD = .23 in the baseline group.

Participants who demonstrated high AIC at baseline — i.e., those adept at formulating effective prompts, filtering responses, and applying outputs — benefited disproportionately from the treatment. In interaction analysis, the coefficient on the (Treatment × AIC) term was positive and significant (p < 0.05).
novice learners (i.e., those with low prior knowledge) benefited more from LLM-assisted study, but only if they had high AIC levels.

Variance in post-intervention exam scores was lower among scaffolded novices (SD = .14) compared to unguided novices (SD = .22), consistent with the view that scaffolding reduces inequality by leveling the floor.
The intervention was conducted at Texas A&M University with a total of 179 participants.
Our experimental setting approximates the conditions faced by early-career knowledge workers adapting to GenAI tools.

result.qual_notes: This study uses a large, nationally representative survey of over 117,000 U.S. firms (the BTOS) to analyze AI diffusion. result.qual_notes: This study uses a staggered difference-in-differences design on proprietary data from over 100,000 engineers at 500 firms. result.one_line: In a randomized experiment, a generative AI assistant substantially increased the performance of all workers on a business problem-solving task, closing about 75% of the productivity gap between low- and high-education individuals.
result.qual_notes: This online experiment randomized 1,174 Argentinian adults into using a GPT-based assistant for a business problem-solving task. The study's main finding is that AI disproportionately benefits lower-education workers, narrowing the performance gap. We address both questions using a randomized online experiment outside firms, in which 1,174 adults aged 25–45 complete an incentivized, workplace style business problem-solving task with or without a generative-AI assistant, followed by a non-AI-assisted follow-up module.

In the control group without AI, higher-education participants outperform lower-education participants by 0.548 standard deviations; with AI, this gap falls to 0.139 standard deviations, closing about three-quarters of the initial gap.

Measured in standard deviations (SD) relative to the low-education control group, Al access increases the overall task score (our main outcome of interest) by 1.242 SD for low-education individuals and by 0.834 SD for high-education individuals.
the treatment closes 75% of the performance gap between low- and high-education participants with no access to the AI assistant.

High-education individuals with access to the AI assistant still outperform their low-education counterparts by 0.139 SD, a large and economically meaningful difference (although marginally insignificant, with p-value=0.110).

By assisting participants in formulating their responses and producing the final written output, AI substitutes for these scarcer inputs and leads to larger productivity gains for this group, even though higher-education participants also benefit.

paper_metadata.title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of Artificial Intelligence on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality
paper_metadata.sample: 758 management consultants at Boston Consulting Group (BCG)

productivity.finding: On tasks within its capability frontier, generative AI increased task completion by 12.2%, speed by 25.1%, and quality by 30-34%, but for a task outside the frontier, it decreased the likelihood of a correct solution by 19 percentage points.

inequality.finding: Generative AI compressed performance inequality among consultants, with those in the bottom half of the baseline skill distribution improving their output quality by 31% compared to an 11% gain for those in the top half.

substitute_complement.finding: AI acts as a powerful complement for tasks within its 'jagged frontier' of capabilities, but workers who substitute it for their own judgment on tasks outside the frontier experience significant performance degradation.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with elite consultants, generative AI boosted performance and reduced inequality on suitable tasks, but led to worse outcomes on tasks outside its capability frontier due to worker overreliance.
result.qual_notes: This field experiment with 758 BCG consultants used GPT-4 to assess performance on realistic tasks designed to be 'inside' or 'outside' the AI's capability frontier. The study introduces the 'jagged frontier' concept, where AI helps on some tasks but hurts on others, with consultants struggling to discern the boundary. A key finding is skill compression: lower-performing consultants benefited much more from AI (+31% quality) than their higher-performing peers (+11% quality). A limitation is the focus on a single firm and the use of subjective quality metrics for some tasks.

result.one_line: In a series of large-scale field experiments at an online retailer, generative AI increased sales by up to 16.3% in consumer-facing workflows like customer service and search, and disproportionately benefited less experienced consumers, thereby reducing performance inequality.
result.qual_notes: The study uses seven randomized field experiments on a large cross-border e-commerce platform to measure GenAI's impact on sales and other business outcomes. Effects vary substantially by workflow, depending on the baseline condition (e.g., no service, human agent, or non-GenAI algorithm). The paper finds strong evidence of complementarity between GenAI and human agents and shows that GenAI compresses performance gaps by helping novice consumers more. The analysis is limited to short-run effects and does not measure labor displacement or long-term changes in consumer behavior.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with knowledge workers, mandating a structured collaborative protocol for using generative AI decreased team output quality and quantity, while training individuals to treat AI as a 'thought partner' increased the odds of producing top-quality work.
result.qual_notes: This field experiment (N=388) at a Fortune 500 retailer tested two interventions: a behavioral protocol for pairs (Task A) and cognitive reframing for individuals (Task B). The behavioral protocol, which required synchronous collaboration, significantly reduced document quality (-4.96 points on a 22-point scale) and completion rates (OR=0.12). The cognitive training, which reframed AI as a partner, increased the odds of producing a perfect-score document by 2.07x in an exploratory analysis. Key limitations include a confound between treatment and time-of-day (AM vs PM sessions), high and differential attrition, and evidence of LLM-grader bias toward longer documents.

result.one_line: In a lab experiment with students, generative AI had no average effect on learning but increased inequality, benefiting high-GPA women while harming low-GPA students, and substituted for conventional study effort.
result.qual_notes: This study was a pre-registered lab experiment (N=572) where students learned a new language (Esperanto) with either no AI (control), unguided AI access (ChatGPT), or guided AI access. The key finding is a null average effect on final exam scores, masking significant heterogeneity: AI-guided access helped high-GPA women achieve top scores but increased the likelihood of low-GPA students getting low scores. AI access also crowded out traditional study, with treated students attempting 12-17% fewer practice questions. An exploratory analysis suggests that students who used AI in a more 'delegative' way (copy-pasting questions) performed worse on the exam.

result.one_line: Using unemployment, LinkedIn, and syllabi data, this paper finds that labor market outcomes for AI-exposed occupations and recent graduates worsened starting in early 2022, before ChatGPT's launch, but that education in LLM-relevant skills improved graduates' outcomes post-ChatGPT.
result.qual_notes: This study analyzes three large-scale US datasets: monthly unemployment insurance records, over 10 million LinkedIn profiles of college graduates, and millions of university syllabi. The key finding is that the deterioration in job prospects for AI-exposed occupations predates the release of ChatGPT, suggesting other macroeconomic factors were at play. For recent graduates, while entering an AI-exposed job post-ChatGPT was associated with lower pay and longer job searches, having an educational background with more exposure to LLM-relevant tasks partially offset these negative effects, pointing to the value of complementary skills.

entry_level.finding: Firms adopting generative AI significantly reduce employment for junior workers, primarily by slowing hiring rather than increasing separations.

result.one_line: Using résumé and job posting data from over 280,000 U.S. firms, this study finds that generative AI adoption leads to a significant decline in employment for junior workers, particularly in AI-exposed occupations, driven primarily by reduced hiring.
result.qual_notes: The study identifies GenAI adoption by detecting firm job postings for 'GenAI integrator' roles. Using a triple-difference design, it finds that junior employment in adopting firms fell by about 9% relative to senior employment after the launch of ChatGPT. This effect is concentrated in high-exposure occupations and is driven by a sharp reduction in hiring, not an increase in separations. The paper finds strong evidence for a task displacement channel (AI substituting for junior tasks) but no evidence for a reallocation channel (juniors taking on more senior tasks). A heterogeneity analysis finds a U-shaped pattern by university prestige, with the largest employment declines for juniors from mid-tier institutions.

Approximately two quarters after adoption, employment begins to decline, reaching an 8 percent reduction after eight quarters.

Specifically, the coefficient on Hiring implies that, relative to non-adopters, GenAI adopting firms hired on average 4.0 fewer junior workers per quarter after 2023Q1— about an 80 percent reduction relative to the pre-period mean of 5.06 hires per firm quarter.

paper_metadata.title: Looking for the Ladder: Is AI Impacting Entry-Level Jobs?
paper_metadata.sample: More than 238 million US job postings from September 2019 to August 2025

entry_level.finding: The paper argues that the recent decline in hiring for entry-level, AI-exposed jobs is not caused by AI displacement but is instead a consequence of macroeconomic factors, specifically monetary policy tightening.

substitute_complement.finding: The paper finds no evidence for AI substituting for human labor, arguing that the timeline of declining job postings in AI-exposed fields predates the widespread availability of generative AI tools.

findings.metric_definition: Visual analysis of the start date of the decline in job postings for AI-exposed occupations.
findings.note: The paper shows graphically that job postings for the most AI-exposed quintile peaked and began a steep decline in Spring 2022, well before the November 2022 launch of ChatGPT, indicating a pre-existing shock.

findings.metric: Relative decline in job postings by seniority
findings.comparison: Junior vs. senior roles in high AI-exposure occupations
findings.metric_definition: Visual comparison of indexed job posting trends for junior and senior roles in the top quintile of AI-exposed occupations.
findings.note: The paper finds no evidence of a differential decline, showing that postings for both junior and senior roles have been falling roughly in parallel since their peak in Spring 2022.

result.one_line: This paper argues that the recent decline in entry-level hiring in AI-exposed occupations is attributable to macroeconomic shocks like interest rate hikes, not displacement by generative AI, as the hiring downturn began months before ChatGPT's release.
result.qual_notes: This paper critiques the finding that generative AI is already displacing entry-level workers. Using US job postings data, the authors argue the timeline is implausible, as the hiring downturn in AI-exposed occupations began six months before ChatGPT's launch. They propose an alternative explanation: these occupations are concentrated in interest-rate-sensitive sectors disproportionately affected by the 2022 monetary policy tightening. The paper also shows that job postings for junior and senior roles declined in parallel, contradicting the narrative of AI specifically replacing the bottom rung of the career ladder.

evidence.snippet: This chart illustrates that job postings for the most AI-exposed quintile (Q5, dark blue line) peaked in Spring 2022 and began a steep decline well before the November 2022 launch of ChatGPT, indicating a pre-existing shock.
There is no evidence that job postings for junior roles within occupations most exposed to AI have declined more than postings for senior positions.
Postings for both levels of seniority have been falling roughly in parallel since their peak in Spring 2022, with the decline in junior positions stabilizing faster.
Our analysis utilizes more than 238 million US job postings (averaging ~3.3 million monthly) from September 2019 to August 2025.

findings.notes: The paper's empirical findings are presented graphically, not as quantitative point estimates. The 'findings' objects are qualitative summaries of these visual results, hence values are null.

substitute_complement.finding: AI substitutes for the human task of conducting interviews, causing recruiters to reallocate their decision-making to weigh standardized test scores more heavily and interview performance less.

entry_level.finding: The study focuses entirely on entry-level jobs, demonstrating that AI can effectively automate the interview screening process for this segment, but does not measure effects on overall demand for these roles.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with over 70,000 job applicants, using a generative AI voice agent to conduct interviews instead of a human recruiter increased job offers by 12% and one-month retention by 18%, with no loss in productivity for hired workers.
result.qual_notes: This large-scale natural field experiment randomized job applicants for entry-level customer service roles in the Philippines to be interviewed by either a human or an AI voice agent. All subsequent hiring decisions were made by human recruiters. The authors attribute the improved hiring outcomes to "controlled variance": the AI conducts more standardized and consistent interviews, which elicits more hiring-relevant information. The study also finds that AI substitutes for the interview task and changes recruiter behavior, causing them to rely more on standardized test scores and take longer to evaluate AI-led interviews.

evidence.snippet: We find that while applicants in the Human Interviewer condition receive a job offer in 8.70% of cases, this fraction significantly increases to 9.73% in the AI Interviewer condition – a 12% higher likelihood of receiving a job offer.

The likelihood of receiving a job offer in the Human Interviewer is 8.70% (1,179 out of 13,557 applicants). In contrast, in the AI Interviewer condition, the likelihood is 9.73% (3,904 out of 40,103 applicants). Consequently, applications that are interviewed by the AI voice agent have a 1.03 percentage point or 12% higher likelihood of receiving a job offer. This difference is significant (p < 0.001, two-sample proportion test).
Importantly, among all applicants randomized into either treatment, applicants in the AI Interviewer condition also have an 18% higher likelihood of starting their job (p < 0.001)
and a 18% higher likelihood of having an employment spell lasting at least one month (p < 0.001).
These positive effects also persist when we look at whether applicants are still employed after two (17% higher likelihood), three (16%), or four months (17%).
Across all three performance variables, we find no significant differences between workers hired in the AI Interviewer condition and those hired in the Human Interviewer condition.

Applicants who selected the AI voice agent scored an average of 3.14 (out of 6) on the language test and 47.54 (out of 100) on the analytical test. In contrast, applicants who chose the human interviewer obtained higher test scores of 3.37 and 49.77 on the language and analytical tests, respectively. These differences are statistically significant (p < 0.001 and p = 0.001, respectively, t-tests).

result.one_line: In a field experiment with 515 startups, providing firms with information on how to map AI into their production processes increased task completion by 12%, revenue by 1.9x, and reduced capital demand by 39.5%, with benefits appearing across all firm types.
result.qual_notes: This study conducted a field experiment with 515 early-stage startups, where treated firms received case studies on reorganizing work around AI. The treatment aimed to solve the 'mapping problem'—discovering where AI can create value. The study finds that this intervention led to more AI adoption, higher task completion, and better firm performance (customer acquisition, revenue), particularly in the upper tail of the distribution. Notably, these performance gains were achieved without increased labor demand and with significantly lower capital demand, and the benefits were not concentrated among firms with higher baseline performance or more technical founders.

inequality.finding: When controlling for remote work, generative AI exposure does not significantly reduce the share of new hires going to junior workers relative to senior workers, suggesting it does not exacerbate inequality along this dimension.

substitute_complement.finding: The paper's main finding implies that generative AI is not a strong substitute for early-career labor, as its apparent displacement effect is an artifact of confounding with remote work.

result.one_line: The observed decline in early-career hiring is primarily driven by the shift to remote work, not generative AI, whose apparent negative effect on junior jobs becomes statistically insignificant when controlling for work-from-home exposure.
result.qual_notes: This study uses two large datasets of new hires and job postings from the US, UK, Canada, and Australia to disentangle the effects of GenAI and Work-From-Home (WFH) on early-career hiring. Using a difference-in-differences design, the authors find that while both GenAI and WFH exposure are individually correlated with a decline in the junior share of hires, the GenAI effect disappears when both are estimated jointly. The paper's central argument is that WFH is a major omitted variable in studies that find large negative effects of GenAI on junior employment.

result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences design around the release of GitHub Copilot X, using GitHub Pro subscription as a proxy for treatment. A supplementary analysis of LinkedIn profiles suggests AI use is associated with a higher likelihood of internal promotions, especially for interns and junior employees.

paper_metadata.title: Labor Demand in the Shadow of Generative AI: Evidence from the U.S. Job Posting Data
paper_metadata.sample: 285 million online job postings from Lightcast in the U.S. during 2018Q1-2025Q2

substitute_complement.finding: After ChatGPT's launch, job postings for occupations with high AI-substitution vulnerability fell by an average of 9.2% relative to low-vulnerability occupations, with the effect intensifying to 14.8% by the third year.

result.one_line: Using US job posting data, this paper finds that the launch of ChatGPT led to a 9% average decline in job postings for occupations highly substitutable by AI, with larger declines for roles requiring more education and experience.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences design on 285 million US job postings, comparing high vs. low AI-substitution occupations after the November 2022 ChatGPT launch. The negative effect on job postings intensifies over time, reaching 15% by the third year. Contrary to some hypotheses, the study finds that within occupations, demand for less-experienced and less-educated workers fell less sharply than for their more credentialed counterparts, suggesting AI may be compressing skill premia. The findings are robust to various controls and also appear in UK and German data.

result.one_line: In a randomized trial with law students, advanced generative AI tools (a reasoning model and a RAG-based system) significantly increased productivity by up to 131% across various legal tasks by improving quality and reducing time, with lower-skilled students benefiting the most.
result.qual_notes: This study was a randomized controlled trial with 137 upper-level law students performing six realistic legal tasks using either a RAG-based tool (Vincent AI), a reasoning model (OpenAI's o1-preview), or no AI. Our study evaluates the performance of 137 law students from the University of Minnesota and the University of Michigan on various legal tasks with or without access to AI tools.
Group size 47 45 45 137
Our findings also show that these AI tools significantly boost productivity in five out of six tested legal tasks, with statistically significant gains of anywhere from 50% to 130%.

AI tools appear to provide the largest gains for participants with lower baseline skill, while offering smaller— and in some cases slightly negative—estimated effects for those with the strongest baseline performance.

To ensure that the six tasks reflected realistic scenarios typically assigned to first- or second-year law firm associates, we developed all of them in collaboration with one or more practicing attorneys.
Participants were encouraged to use AI as an aid to enhance their work rather than as a substitute for their own judgment.
Instead, the results suggest that access to cutting-edge Al systems primarily “raise the floor," narrowing performance gaps rather than uniformly shifting the entire distribution upward.

paper_metadata.title: How AI Impacts Skill Formation
paper_metadata.sample: 52 professional software developers

productivity.finding: On average, AI assistance did not significantly improve task completion time for a learning-focused coding task, but it did significantly reduce skill formation as measured by a 17% lower quiz score.

inequality.finding: AI-assisted learning reduced skill formation across all tested levels of developer experience, suggesting the risk of skill atrophy is not confined to novices.

entry_level.finding: AI assistance impairs the formation of conceptual understanding and debugging skills for novice workers learning a new tool, potentially compromising a key mechanism for on-the-job skill acquisition.

substitute_complement.finding: Workers use AI as a substitute for cognitive effort (delegation) or a complement to their learning (inquiry), with delegation leading to the worst skill outcomes.

result.one_line: In a randomized experiment with software developers learning a new library, AI assistance did not significantly increase task completion speed but impaired skill formation, reducing subsequent quiz scores by 17% on average.
result.qual_notes: The study (N=52) randomly assigned professional software developers to learn a new programming library with or without a GPT-4O assistant. While AI did not significantly change task completion time on average, it significantly reduced subsequent skill retention, measured by a 17% lower score on a quiz covering conceptual, debugging, and code-reading skills. This negative effect depended heavily on usage patterns: developers who delegated tasks to the AI learned the least (39% quiz score), while those using it for conceptual inquiry or to understand generated code learned as much or more than the control group (65-86% scores). The findings suggest AI can hinder on-the-job learning if it encourages cognitive offloading.

result.one_line: In US industries most exposed to generative AI, the introduction of ChatGPT was followed by an immediate and persistent 9-13% decline in hiring of early-career workers (ages 22-24), leading to a 12% drop in their employment over the next 10 quarters.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences event study design on near-universal US private sector administrative data (QWI) to compare outcomes in the most AI-exposed industry-state cells to less-exposed ones. The primary finding is a sharp decline in hiring and employment for workers aged 22-24 immediately following ChatGPT's release, with much smaller effects on older workers. The paper finds that this is driven by a reduction in both job gains at growing firms and replacement hires. A triple-difference analysis suggests some pre-existing negative trends for young workers in these industries, potentially related to remote work, but finds a discontinuous shift in hiring practices post-ChatGPT.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with customer service workers, an agentic AI increased speed on AI-eligible tasks by 16.8% but lowered customer ratings, while also freeing up workers to improve speed and quality on more complex, non-AI tasks.
result.qual_notes: This randomized field experiment at Alibaba involved 647 customer service workers, where the treatment group supervised an agentic LLM on 'AI-eligible' chats while also handling 'AI-ineligible' chats. The study finds that while AI speeds up simple tasks, it harms subjective quality unless a human intervenes. The effectiveness of human intervention is highly context-dependent, working well for technical issues but failing for emotionally escalated chats. A key finding is the positive spillover: by offloading simple tasks, AI allows workers to devote more focused attention to complex tasks, improving both speed and quality on that work.

paper_metadata.title: The Impact of Large Language Models on Open-Source Innovation: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
paper_metadata.sample: 1,187 matched pairs of Python (treated) and R (control) open-source packages from GitHub.

productivity.finding: GitHub Copilot increased the volume of open-source contributions by 28-40%, demonstrating a significant productivity gain for developers.

substitute_complement.finding: AI complements incremental, maintenance-oriented coding tasks more than substantive, feature-creating tasks, shifting the composition of innovation toward exploitation of existing codebases.

result.one_line: GitHub Copilot increased open-source developer contributions by 28-40%, with the gains disproportionately concentrated in incremental maintenance tasks rather than the creation of new, substantive features.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences design, exploiting GitHub Copilot's initial support for Python but not R as a natural experiment. The analysis of over 1.1 million commits to 1,187 matched package pairs finds that AI's productivity boost is larger for more routine, incremental tasks (e.g., maintenance, bug fixes) than for substantive tasks (e.g., adding new functions). This effect is more pronounced in projects with higher activity (more context for the AI) and widens after a Copilot model upgrade, suggesting a structural shift toward exploitation over exploration in open-source innovation.

evidence.snippet: GitHub Copilot increases commits by 6.816 per quarter in the TWFE specification (p<0.001), a 39.55% increase relative to the pre-treatment mean of the treated group of 17.235 commits per quarter (Angrist and Pischke 2009).

paper_metadata.sample: Nearly 6,000 senior business executives at US, UK, German, and Australian firms (N=5,888), and a separate survey of US employees (N≈3,000).

productivity.finding: Executives report a small 0.29% productivity boost from AI over the past three years but anticipate a much larger 1.44% increase over the next three years.

substitute_complement.finding: Executives expect AI to substitute for labor, forecasting an average own-firm employment reduction of 0.68% over the next three years, a sharp contrast to employees who expect a 0.45% increase.

entry_level.finding: Firms expecting to reduce employment due to AI anticipate that around two-thirds of this reduction will come from hiring fewer new employees rather than from laying off existing ones.

result.one_line: Surveying nearly 6,000 executives, this study finds that while AI has had minimal impact on productivity (+0.3%) and employment (0%) to date, firms anticipate significant future effects, expecting a 1.4% productivity boost but a 0.7% reduction in employment over the next three years.
result.qual_notes: This study is based on large-scale surveys of senior business executives at nearly 6,000 firms in the US, UK, Germany, and Australia, conducted between late 2025 and early 2026. The findings represent firms' self-reported realized impacts and, more centrally, their expectations for the next three years. A key finding is the divergence in expectations between executives, who foresee net job substitution (-0.7% employment), and employees, who anticipate net job creation (+0.5%). The expected employment reduction is projected to come primarily from reduced hiring rather than layoffs.

evidence.snippet: We survey nearly 6,000 senior business executives at US, UK, German, and Australian firms to develop new evidence on AI adoption and its effects on jobs, productivity, and output.
Around two-thirds of the reduction in employment is expected to come from firms hiring fewer new employees.
Around 3,000 people answered.
